request.question: Scott Howell is a consultant who has worked with the mayor of what city?

title: David Cruz Thayne
David Morgan (psychoanalyst)
Matilda Howell
Rudy Giuliani
Jun Choi
Pete Ashdown
Howell School
Scott Howell (footballer)
Scott Howell (political consultant)
United States Senate election in Utah, 2012
sentences: David Cruz Thayne (March 10, 1971) is an American small businessman, former professional tennis player, and Democratic congressional candidate.

He formerly served as chief of staff and campaign manager to Utah State Senate Minority Leader Scott Howell, a Democrat, and currently operates his own tennis-oriented small business in Southern California which has also led to the production of two tennis oriented documentaries.
He was a candidate for California’s 26th Congressional District in the United States House of Representatives elections in California, 2012.
David Howell Morgan (BAHons D.Clin.Psych.
FBPAS FPBPA SMBPF.
Training Analyst Supervisor IPA.
AMBPS.
MBPC) is a psychoanalyst and consultant psychotherapist who has worked at the Portman Clinic London for 25 years.
He went on to specialise in patients' presenting with problems around sexuality and violence.
Morgan co-edited a popular textbook on sexuality, perversion and delinquency.
He is now a psychoanalyst and consultant seeing private patients and provides consultations to political groups.
He is chair of the Political Minds Seminars at the British Psychoanalytic Society.
He regularly contributes to radio and television and lectures nationally and internationally.

Morgan’s recent research has focused on the psychological implications for whistleblowers and the conditions, socially, politically, and psychologically under which a person may "turn a blind eye" or take on the responsibility of whistleblowing.
He also presents a podcast called "Frontier Psychoanalyst" on BBC i-player.
Lida Scott Howell (August 28, 1859 – December 20, 1938) was an American female archer who competed in the early twentieth century.
She won three gold medals in Archery at the 1904 Summer Olympics in Missouri in the double national and Columbia rounds and for the US team.

Rudolph William Louis Giuliani {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} ( ; born May 28, 1944) is an American lawyer, businessman, public speaker, former mayor of New York City, and an informal adviser on cybersecurity to the White House.
Jun H. Choi (born May 17, 1971) is an American politician and the former Mayor of Edison, New Jersey, a community of over 100,000 people and the fifth largest municipality in the state.
He was sworn in on January 1, 2006 as the youngest mayor in Edison history and the first Asian American mayor of a major city in New Jersey.
He began a good government, reform movement that led to greater fiscal responsibility, economic development, public education improvements, environmental protection and civic engagement.
Prior to becoming Mayor of Edison, Choi worked in both business and government.

He served as a senior official on education policy at the New Jersey Department of Education where he started the NJ SMART Program, improving public school education performance metrics for 1.4 million New Jersey children.
He also served in the White House Office of Management and Budget and worked as a management consultant focusing on strategy and technology issues for Fortune 500 US corporations.
Peter Lynn "Pete" Ashdown (born January 11, 1967) is the founder and CEO of Utah's first independent and oldest Internet service provider, XMission.
Running as the Democratic candidate in 2006, Ashdown lost to incumbent U.S. Senator Orrin Hatch.
Ashdown ran again in 2012 for the U.S. Senate, but was defeated in his pursuit of the Democratic nomination at the state convention by Scott Howell.
Howell School is a historic school building in Dothan, Alabama.
The school was built in 1902 to replace the previous grammar school that had burned in 1901.
It was named in 1916 for the recently deceased Dr. Robert Graves Howell, who was mayor when the first grammar school was built.
A second school was built in 1911, and Howell was replaced by Minnie T. Heard Elementary School in 1942.
After being used by the Salvation Army during World War II, the building was converted for use as a textile mill in 1947.
A number of companies operated out of the building until 1952, when the Dothan Manufacturing Company took over the property.
A one-story addition was constructed on the front of the building in 1965, obscuring the façade.
The plant closed in 1997, and was sold to the Southern Alabama Regional Council on the Aging in 2003, before the city re-acquired it in 2008.
Scott Howell (born 10 May 1958) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Carlton in the VFL during the early 1980s.
Herbert Weston Scott Howell III is an American conservative political consultant, whose recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani.

The 2012 United States Senate election in Utah took place on November 6, 2012, concurrently with the 2012 U.S. presidential election as well as other elections to the United States Senate and House of Representatives and as various state and local elections.
Incumbent Republican U.S. Senator Orrin Hatch won re-election to a seventh term against former state Senator and IBM executive, Scott Howell the Democratic candidate.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Scott Howell is a consultant who has worked with the mayor of what city?
hop1_docs: Scott Howell (political consultant) | Herbert Weston Scott Howell III is an American conservative political consultant, whose recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani.

Scott Smith (American politician) | Scott Smith (born 1956) is an American businessman and politician, elected as the 38th mayor of Mesa, Arizona, on May 20, 2008.  He took office on June 2, 2008 and served until April 16, 2014, when he resigned to run for Governor of Arizona in the Republican Primary.  In 2013-14, he was president of the United States Conference of Mayors.  He was previously president of both Great Western Homes and K. Hovnanian Homes.  He has also worked in finance and as a business consultant.
Scott Howell (footballer) | Scott Howell (born 10 May 1958) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Carlton in the VFL during the early 1980s.

Scott Howell (politician) | Scott N. Howell (born September 28, 1953) is an American politician and Utah who was a candidate for the United States Senate in the 2012 election.  He is a member of the Democratic Party.

Municipality of Isfahan | The municipality of Isfahan is a non-governmental organization, which is responsible for the management of the city of Isfahan.  The highest-ranking official of this organization is the mayor of Isfahan, who is chosen by the city council of Isfahan.  The city of Isfahan has 15 administrative divisions and each division has its own mayor, who work under the mayor of Isfahan.  The present mayor of Isfahan is Mehdi Jamalinejad.

City Mayors Foundation | The City Mayors Foundation, also known as City Mayors, is an international think tank dedicated to urban affairs.  It has been active since 2003 and runs the biennial World Mayor award, as well as providing pro bono consultancy services.  Unlike Eurocities and United Cities and Local Governments it is wholly independent of any city.

W. Ray Scott | Walter Ray Scott, Sr. (March 19, 1923 – February 23, 2008), usually known as W. Ray Scott, was from 1960 to 1976 the Democratic mayor of Natchitoches, the oldest city in Louisiana.  As mayor, he is primarily remembered for his work with the administration of Governor Edwin Washington Edwards in procuring physical expansion and university status for Northwestern State University, formerly Northwestern State College.  In addition, he worked to establish the municipal water supply from Sibley Lake in Natchitoches.  From 1956 to 1960, Scott was the elected municipal finance commissioner.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "Scott Howell (political consultant)" — passage [1] quotes: "Herbert Weston Scott Howell III" and "recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani." (passage [1])
  - Other name candidates in the passages: "Scott Howell (footballer)" — passage [3] quotes "born 10 May 1958." (passage [3]); "Scott N. Howell" / "Scott Howell (politician)" — passage [4] quotes "born September 28, 1953." (passage [4]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Multiple Scott Howell pages exist in the passages: "Scott Howell (political consultant)" (passage [1]), "Scott Howell (footballer)" (passage [3]), and "Scott N. Howell / Scott Howell (politician)" (passage [4]). Do not assume which Scott Howell is intended without confirming the consultant page.
  - The passages list "Rudy Giuliani" as a client but do not include Giuliani's own page or any statement here that names the city he served as mayor.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "Rudy Giuliani".
  - Missing fact needed to answer the question: the name of the city that Rudy Giuliani served as mayor of (i.e., "Mayor of <city>").
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Rudy Giuliani" page — check the lede and infobox for any "Mayor of" office (exact city name) and the 'Political career' or 'Offices held' sections. Search keywords: "Rudy Giuliani lede infobox 'Mayor of'".
  2) Open "Scott Howell (political consultant)" page (passage [1]) — confirm the client mention in the lede/Clients section to verify the link to Giuliani. Search keywords: "Scott Howell political consultant clients Giuliani".
  3) If needed, open "Scott Howell" disambiguation or the other Scott Howell pages ("Scott Howell (footballer)", "Scott N. Howell") to ensure the consultant page is the correct subject.
- Final note: Do not provide the final answer yet — retrieve the above pages/sections to verify which city Rudy Giuliani was mayor of before answering.
hop2_docs: Mayoralty of Rudy Giuliani | Rudy Giuliani (full name "Rudolph William Louis Giuliani") served as the 107th Mayor of New York City from January 1, 1994 until December 31, 2001.
Electoral history of Rudy Giuliani | Electoral history of Rudy Giuliani, 107th Mayor of New York City and was a candidate for the 2008 Republican presidential nomination.

Rudy Giuliani promotions of Bernard Kerik | Rudy Giuliani, as mayor of the City of New York, promoted the career of Bernard Kerik (by appointment or recommendation) on several occasions.  Critics have charged that Giuliani showed consistently poor judgment in doing so.
Rudy Washington | Rudy Washington is an American politician who served as deputy mayor under New York City former mayor Rudy Giuliani.
Giuliani Time | Giuliani Time is a 2005 documentary film by Kevin Keating about Rudy Giuliani, former Mayor of New York City.
Helen Giuliani | Helen Giuliani (née D'Avanzo; September 26, 1909 – September 8 2002) was the mother of former New York City Mayor Rudy Giuliani.

Rudy Giuliani: Urban Legend | Rudy Giuliani: Urban Legend is a video produced by the International Association of Firefighters (IAFF).  On July 11, 2007, the IAFF released the 13-minute video in DVD format to fire departments across the U.S.  The DVD outlines its complaints against Rudy Giuliani.  It is critical of the 2008 Republican Party presidential candidate and former New York City mayor.  As the video has been issued on a website, and not just DVD, it is classifiable as a viral video.
hop2_titles: Rudy Giuliani promotions of Bernard Kerik
